🎯
All Issues — Priority Order
Fix in this order for maximum score gain
# Issue Affects Score Impact
1 LCP = 5.1s → fix to <2.5s Mobile −18 pts
2 Improve image delivery (81 KiB mobile / 335 KiB desktop) Both −5 pts
3 Render-blocking requests Both −3 pts
4 Legacy JavaScript (14 KiB) Both −2 pts
5 Reduce unused JavaScript (27 KiB) Both −2 pts
6 Minimize main-thread work (6.2s) Mobile −2 pts
7 Touch targets too small Both Accessibility
8 Document missing main landmark Both Accessibility
⚡
Fix #1 — LCP: 5.1s → Under 2.5s (CRITICAL)
LCP 5.1s
CRITICAL
Preload your hero image — LCP element
Fixes: LCP request discovery + LCP 5.1s

The LCP element (your hero image) is discovered too late. The browser finds it only after parsing CSS/JS. You must tell the browser about it immediately in <head>.

pages/_document.tsx (or app/layout.tsx)
// In your <Head> — add BEFORE any other resource
<link
  rel="preload"
  as="image"
  href="/images/hero.webp"
  type="image/webp"
  fetchpriority="high"
/>

// If responsive, include srcset:
<link
  rel="preload"
  as="image"
  imagesrcset="/images/hero-480.webp 480w, /images/hero-800.webp 800w, /images/hero.webp 1200w"
  imagesizes="100vw"
  fetchpriority="high"
/>
Your hero <Image> component — add priority prop
// WRONG — no priority:
<Image src="/images/hero.webp" alt="Hero" />

// CORRECT — always add priority to LCP image:
<Image
  src="/images/hero.webp"
  alt="Hero"
  priority              // disables lazy-load, adds fetchpriority=high
  width={1200}
  height={630}
  sizes="100vw"
/>
✅ Rule: The first visible image on every page MUST have priority. Never lazy-load the LCP element.
CRITICAL
Network Dependency Tree — reduce request chain depth
Fixes: Network dependency tree warning

Critical resources are loading in a chain: HTML → CSS → Font → Image. Each step adds full RTT. Break the chain by preconnecting and preloading.

_document.tsx — add preconnects for all 3rd-party origins
<Head>
  // Step 1: Preconnect to all external origins you use
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossOrigin="" />
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://cdn.yourdomain.com" />

  // Step 2: Preload hero image immediately after
  <link rel="preload" as="image" href="/images/hero.webp" fetchpriority="high" />
</Head>
next.config.js — self-host Google Fonts (best approach)
// BEST: Use next/font instead of Google Fonts <link>
// This eliminates the external font request entirely

// app/layout.tsx or pages/_app.tsx
import { Syne } from 'next/font/google'

const syne = Syne({
  subsets: ['latin'],
  weight: ['400', '600', '700'],
  display: 'swap',   // prevents invisible text flash
  preload: true,    // auto-preloads the font file
})

export default function RootLayout({ children }) {
  return (
    <html>
      <body className={syne.className}>{children}</body>
    </html>
  )
}
🖼
Fix #2 — Image Delivery (335 KiB desktop / 81 KiB mobile)
−335 KiB
HIGH
Convert all images to WebP/AVIF + proper sizing
Est. savings: 335 KiB desktop, 81 KiB mobile

Next.js Image handles this automatically — but ONLY if you use <Image> correctly with sizes prop. Wrong sizes = oversized images served to mobile.

next.config.js — enable AVIF + configure domains
/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
const nextConfig = {
  images: {
    formats: ['image/avif', 'image/webp'], // AVIF first = smaller files
    deviceSizes: [320, 480, 640, 828, 1080, 1200, 1920],
    imageSizes: [16, 32, 48, 64, 96, 128, 256],
    minimumCacheTTL: 60 * 60 * 24 * 30, // cache 30 days
    dangerouslyAllowSVG: false,
    contentSecurityPolicy: "default-src 'self'; script-src 'none'; sandbox;",
  },
}
module.exports = nextConfig
Component — correct sizes prop (critical for mobile savings)
// ❌ WRONG — serves 1200px image on 375px mobile phone:
<Image src="/hero.jpg" fill />

// ✅ CORRECT — tells browser actual rendered size per breakpoint:

// Full-width hero image:
<Image
  src="/images/hero.webp"
  alt="Hero banner"
  fill
  sizes="100vw"
  priority
  style={{ objectFit: 'cover' }}
/>

// Card image (half-width on desktop, full on mobile):
<Image
  src="/images/card.webp"
  alt="Card"
  width={600}
  height={400}
  sizes="(max-width: 768px) 100vw, 50vw"
/>

// Small icon/thumbnail (always small):
<Image
  src="/icons/logo.webp"
  alt="Logo"
  width={120}
  height={40}
  sizes="120px"
/>
Batch convert existing images to WebP (run once)
# Install sharp CLI
npm install -g sharp-cli

# Convert all JPG/PNG in public/images to WebP
for f in public/images/*.{jpg,jpeg,png}; do
  sharp -i "$f" -o "${f%.*}.webp"
done

# Or use squoosh-cli for AVIF batch conversion
npx @squoosh/cli --avif auto public/images/*.jpg
✅ Pro tip: Never use raw <img> tags in Next.js. Always use next/image. It handles WebP/AVIF conversion, lazy loading, and responsive srcsets automatically.
🚧
Fix #3 — Render-Blocking Requests
Blocks FCP
HIGH
Eliminate or defer all render-blocking CSS/JS
Fixes: Render-blocking requests warning

Any <link stylesheet> or <script> in <head> without async/defer blocks the browser from painting anything. The page appears white until they finish downloading.

_document.tsx — defer non-critical scripts
// ❌ WRONG — blocks render:
<script src="https://cdn.example.com/analytics.js"></script>

// ✅ CORRECT — use next/script with strategy:
import Script from 'next/script'

// Analytics, chat widgets, tag managers → afterInteractive
<Script
  src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/gtag/js"
  strategy="afterInteractive"   // loads after hydration
/>

// Social widgets, non-critical SDKs → lazyOnload
<Script
  src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js"
  strategy="lazyOnload"   // loads when browser is idle
/>

// Inline scripts that MUST run before page renders → beforeInteractive
<Script id="theme-init" strategy="beforeInteractive">
  {`document.documentElement.dataset.theme = localStorage.getItem('theme') || 'light'`}
</Script>
Non-critical CSS — load asynchronously
// Fonts loaded via <link> block render — use next/font instead
// But if you MUST use a CSS file (e.g. third-party widget CSS):

<link
  rel="stylesheet"
  href="/css/non-critical.css"
  media="print"           // browser skips for screen render
  onLoad="this.media='all'"  // then applies it after load
/>
<noscript>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/non-critical.css"/>
</noscript>
⚠️ Watch out: Any CSS imported in your _app.tsx or layout.tsx that is NOT in globals.css may create extra blocking stylesheets. Keep critical styles in globals.css and split the rest using CSS modules.
📦
Fix #4 & #5 — Legacy JS + Unused JS
−41 KiB
HIGH
Target modern browsers + eliminate polyfills
Fixes: Legacy JavaScript — Est savings 14 KiB

Next.js is shipping polyfills for old browsers. Since your site is a view-only website for modern users, target modern browsers only.

.browserslistrc — create this file in project root
# Target modern browsers only (covers 95%+ of users)
last 2 Chrome versions
last 2 Firefox versions
last 2 Safari versions
last 2 Edge versions
not IE 11
not dead
next.config.js — enable modern JS output
const nextConfig = {
  experimental: {
    legacyBrowsers: false,   // Next.js 12: removes legacy bundles
    browsersListForSwc: true, // uses .browserslistrc in SWC
  },
  swcMinify: true,           // SWC minifier (faster + better)
}
HIGH
Dynamic imports + code splitting for unused JS
Fixes: Reduce unused JavaScript — Est savings 27 KiB

JavaScript that is downloaded but never executed on the current page wastes bandwidth and blocks the main thread. Split heavy components and libraries.

Lazy-load heavy components
import dynamic from 'next/dynamic'

// Heavy map, chart, modal, slider etc → load on demand
const HeavySlider = dynamic(
  () => import('../components/HeavySlider'),
  {
    loading: () => <div className="skeleton" />,
    ssr: false  // if not needed for SSR / SEO
  }
)

// Load only when user scrolls to that section:
const ContactForm = dynamic(() => import('../components/ContactForm'))

// GOOD CANDIDATES to dynamic import on a view-only site:
// - Image galleries / lightboxes
// - Video players
// - Maps
// - Any component below the fold
Analyse your bundle — find what's causing the bloat
# Install bundle analyser
npm install --save-dev @next/bundle-analyzer

# next.config.js
const withBundleAnalyzer = require('@next/bundle-analyzer')({
  enabled: process.env.ANALYZE === 'true',
})
module.exports = withBundleAnalyzer(nextConfig)

# Run it:
ANALYZE=true npm run build

# Look for: large libraries you can replace
# moment.js → date-fns (lighter)
# lodash → lodash-es with tree-shaking
# any library over 50kb you only use 1 function from
🧵
Fix #6 — Minimize Main-Thread Work (6.2s)
6.2s
HIGH
Long tasks blocking mobile CPU — 20 long tasks found
Fixes: Minimize main-thread work + Avoid long main-thread tasks

Mobile CPUs are ~4-5x slower than desktop. 20 long tasks (>50ms each) means the browser is frozen for 6 seconds on mobile. This is where most of your mobile score loss comes from.

Use Intersection Observer for below-fold content
// Don't render heavy sections until they're visible
import { useRef, useState, useEffect } from 'react'

function LazySection({ children }) {
  const [visible, setVisible] = useState(false)
  const ref = useRef(null)

  useEffect(() => {
    const obs = new IntersectionObserver(
      ([entry]) => { if (entry.isIntersecting) { setVisible(true); obs.disconnect() } },
      { rootMargin: '200px' }   // start loading 200px before visible
    )
    if (ref.current) obs.observe(ref.current)
    return () => obs.disconnect()
  }, [])

  return <div ref={ref}>{visible ? children : <div style={{minHeight: '200px'}} />}</div>
}
Avoid expensive animations with non-composited properties
/* ❌ WRONG — triggers layout + paint (main thread): */
.animate {
  transition: width 0.3s, height 0.3s, top 0.3s, left 0.3s;
  animation: fadeInUp 0.5s;
}
@keyframes fadeInUp {
  from { top: 20px; opacity: 0 }   /* ❌ top triggers layout */
}

/* ✅ CORRECT — GPU-composited only (off main thread): */
.animate {
  transition: transform 0.3s, opacity 0.3s;  /* only transform + opacity */
  will-change: transform;  /* hint browser to put on GPU layer */
}
@keyframes fadeInUp {
  from { transform: translateY(20px); opacity: 0 }  /* ✅ transform = GPU */
  to   { transform: translateY(0); opacity: 1 }
}

/* NEVER use will-change on everything — only elements that animate */
Use React.memo + useMemo for expensive components
import { memo, useMemo } from 'react'

// Prevent re-render of static content sections
const StaticSection = memo(function StaticSection({ data }) {
  const processed = useMemo(() => data.map(expensiveTransform), [data])
  return <div>{processed.map(item => <Card key={item.id} {...item} />)}</div>
})
✅ Key insight for view-only sites: Since there's no dynamic data, use getStaticProps / generateStaticParams to pre-render everything at build time. Static HTML pages load in under 200ms with no JS execution cost.
♿
Fix #7 & #8 — Accessibility: 94 → 100
94 → 100
MEDIUM
Touch targets too small — minimum 44×44px
Affects: Mobile tap targets (buttons, links, icons)

Google requires all interactive elements to be at least 44×44px with adequate spacing. Small icons and inline links fail this check.

globals.css — global touch target minimum
/* Apply minimum touch target to all interactive elements */
button,
a,
[role="button"],
input[type="checkbox"],
input[type="radio"],
select {
  min-height: 44px;
  min-width: 44px;
  display: inline-flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
}

/* For icon-only buttons that must stay visually small: */
.icon-btn {
  position: relative;
  width: 24px;
  height: 24px;
  /* Expand tap area without changing visual size */
}
.icon-btn::after {
  content: '';
  position: absolute;
  inset: -10px;  /* extends clickable area 10px in all directions */
}

/* Nav links need spacing between them too */
nav a {
  padding: 10px 12px;  /* ensures 44px height + spacing */
  min-height: 44px;
}
Component — correct button with touch target
// ❌ WRONG — icon button with no padding:
<button>
  <svg width="18" height="18">...</svg>
</button>

// ✅ CORRECT — 44px minimum with visible focus ring:
<button
  style={{
    width: 44, height: 44,
    display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', justifyContent: 'center',
    borderRadius: 8, cursor: 'pointer',
    outline: 'none',
  }}
  aria-label="Close menu"      // REQUIRED for icon-only buttons
  onFocus={e => e.target.style.outline = '2px solid #4fa8ff'}
  onBlur={e => e.target.style.outline = 'none'}
>
  <svg width="18" height="18" aria-hidden="true">...</svg>
</button>
MEDIUM
Document does not have a main landmark
Affects: Screen readers, ARIA roles, Lighthouse accessibility score

Screen readers use landmark roles to navigate pages. Without a <main> element, they can't skip to main content. This is a 1-minute fix with large accessibility impact.

Every page layout — wrap content in semantic HTML
// ❌ WRONG — div soup, no landmarks:
export default function Layout({ children }) {
  return (
    <div className="layout">
      <div className="nav">...</div>
      <div className="content">{children}</div>
      <div className="footer">...</div>
    </div>
  )
}

// ✅ CORRECT — proper semantic landmarks:
export default function Layout({ children }) {
  return (
    <div className="layout">
      <header role="banner">          {/* site header / nav */}
        <nav aria-label="Main navigation">...</nav>
      </header>

      <main id="main-content" role="main">   {/* ← THIS IS THE FIX */}
        {children}
      </main>

      <footer role="contentinfo">...</footer>
    </div>
  )
}

// BONUS: Add skip-to-main link (required for WCAG AA):
<a
  href="#main-content"
  className="skip-link"
  >
  Skip to main content
</a>

/* globals.css — hide skip link visually but keep accessible */
.skip-link {
  position: absolute;
  top: -100%;
  left: 0;
  background: #000;
  color: #fff;
  padding: 12px 20px;
  z-index: 9999;
}
.skip-link:focus { top: 0; }
🚀
Bonus — Static Site Supercharger (view-only sites)
+10 pts free
BONUS
Enable full static export + HTTP/2 push headers
Free performance gains for view-only websites

For a view-only Next.js site with no backend, full static export gives you near-instant load times. You also get CDN edge caching for free.

next.config.js — enable static export
const nextConfig = {
  output: 'export',          // generates /out folder, pure static HTML
  trailingSlash: true,      // /about → /about/index.html
  images: {
    unoptimized: false,     // keep optimization ON (Vercel/Netlify handle it)
    formats: ['image/avif', 'image/webp'],
  },
}
vercel.json — HTTP headers for caching + security
{
  "headers": [
    {
      "source": "/(.*)",
      "headers": [
        { "key": "X-Content-Type-Options", "value": "nosniff" },
        { "key": "X-Frame-Options", "value": "DENY" },
        { "key": "X-XSS-Protection", "value": "1; mode=block" },
        { "key": "Referrer-Policy", "value": "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" },
        { "key": "Permissions-Policy", "value": "camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()" }
      ]
    },
    {
      "source": "/images/(.*)",
      "headers": [
        { "key": "Cache-Control", "value": "public, max-age=31536000, immutable" }
      ]
    },
    {
      "source": "/_next/static/(.*)",
      "headers": [
        { "key": "Cache-Control", "value": "public, max-age=31536000, immutable" }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
next.config.js — HTTP/2 Link preload headers (Vercel)
const nextConfig = {
  async headers() {
    return [
      {
        source: '/',
        headers: [
          {
            key: 'Link',
            // HTTP/2 push: tell CDN to push hero image before browser asks
            value: '</images/hero.webp>; rel=preload; as=image',
          },
        ],
      },
    ]
  },
}
✅
Pre-Delivery Checklist

Performance

Hero image has priority prop — next/image priority on LCP element
All images use next/image with sizes prop — no raw <img> tags
Images converted to WebP/AVIF — avif first in formats array
Fonts use next/font/google — not <link> to Google Fonts
All 3rd-party scripts use next/script — strategy="afterInteractive" or "lazyOnload"
Heavy components use dynamic import — galleries, maps, sliders below fold
.browserslistrc targets modern browsers — removes polyfills
Animations use only transform + opacity — GPU composited, no layout triggers
Bundle analyzer run — no unexpected large packages

Accessibility

<main> wraps all page content — in every page/layout
<header>, <nav>, <footer> used correctly — semantic HTML throughout
All buttons min 44×44px — padding or ::after expansion
Icon-only buttons have aria-label — menu, close, social icons
All images have alt text — descriptive for content images, alt="" for decorative
Nav links have 8px+ spacing — prevents touch target overlap
Skip-to-main link added — visible on focus
Focus styles visible — never outline:none without replacement
Color contrast 4.5:1 minimum — text on backgrounds

Final Test Protocol

Run Lighthouse on production URL — not localhost, not preview
Test in Incognito mode — no extensions affecting scores
Run mobile test separately — Lighthouse → Device: Mobile
Run 3 times, average the scores — Lighthouse varies ±3 pts
Test on PageSpeed Insights — pagespeed.web.dev (uses real-world data)

Target After All Fixes

Desktop & Mobile — all categories

100
Performance
100
Accessibility
100
Best Practices
100
SEO
LCP target: <1.5s  |  FCP target: <1.0s  |  TBT target: <50ms  |  CLS target: 0
